---
title: "4.1 Ethical and Social Issues Around Data Collection"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Ethical and Social Issues Around Data Collection

### Unit 4 starts with the human consequences of storing and using data

Before students work with arrays and data sets, the textbook frames the question:

::: {.tight-list}
- what personal data is being collected?
- who can access it?
- what can go wrong when programs use incomplete or biased data?
:::

## Data Privacy

### Useful software often depends on personal data

![Location history and personal-data tracking](../../assets/4-data-collections/4-1-ethical-and-social-issues-around-data-collection/googletimeline.png){fig-align="center" width="78%"}

Apps for maps, driving directions, purchases, or games may collect:

::: {.tight-list}
- location history
- device or usage activity
- purchase behavior
- other personal records linked to the user
:::

## Privacy, Security, and Permission

### Data collection can be beneficial and harmful at the same time

The textbook keeps these ideas together:

::: {.tight-list}
- location tracking can help users navigate or be found in an emergency
- the same data can create privacy and security risks
- programmers should <span class="term">safeguard user privacy</span>
- users should be asked for permission before data is accessed or stored
- data breaches require disclosure to affected users
:::

## Data and Bias

### Biased data can produce unfair program outcomes

The textbook uses AI and machine learning as a concrete context.

::: {.tight-list}
- internet-scale data may already contain social bias
- systems trained on that data may repeat or amplify the bias
- this can affect hiring, facial recognition, search results, and other decisions
:::

<span class="term">Algorithmic bias</span> means repeated program errors that create unfair outcomes for a group of users.

## Dataset Quality Matters

### Not every data set is suitable for every conclusion

Students should evaluate a data set before using it to draw conclusions.

::: {.tight-list}
- some data sets are incomplete
- some contain inaccurate data
- some are appropriate only for one topic or question
- using weak data can make a program incorrect, inefficient, or unfair
:::

Mitigation strategies include representative data, bias testing, transparency, and diverse development teams.

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- discuss when location tracking is beneficial or harmful
- evaluate privacy risks in everyday software
- explain what makes a data set biased or incomplete
- analyze how biased data can create unfair outputs
- use the short-answer reflection activities on privacy, bias, and dataset quality
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain why personal privacy is at risk when programs collect user data
- describe both beneficial and harmful impacts of data collection
- define <span class="term">algorithmic bias</span>
- explain why incomplete or inaccurate data can produce weak outcomes
- describe at least one way programmers can reduce bias or protect privacy
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
